title: 🚸 School Bus & Van Driver Training
description: Specialized safety training for school transport drivers focusing on child safety, route management, and emergency protocols for transporting students.

units.title: Child Passenger Safety
units.content:
# Child Passenger Safety

## Age-Appropriate Restraints

### Children Under 4 Years
- Must use child safety seat
- Rear-facing as long as possible
- Proper installation critical
- Harness straps at or below shoulders
- Chest clip at armpit level

### Children 4-8 Years
- Booster seat required by law
- Positions seat belt correctly
- Lap belt low on hips
- Shoulder belt across chest
- Not behind back or under arm

### Children 8-12 Years
- Seat belt properly positioned
- Lap belt low on hips
- Shoulder belt across chest
- Back against seat
- Feet flat on floor

## School Bus Specific Safety

### Seat Belt Usage
- School buses may have lap belts
- Ensure all belts fastened
- Check before departure
- Report damaged belts
- Document belt usage

### Loading Zone Safety
- Danger zone extends 10 feet around bus
- Children should stay 10 feet away
- Driver must see all children
- Count children before moving
- Never move if child is near bus

## Special Needs Considerations

### Physical Disabilities
- Secure wheelchairs properly
- Use approved restraints
- Allow extra boarding time
- Have emergency plan for each child
- Communicate with parents

### Behavioral Issues
- Know each child's needs
- Have behavior management plan
- Use positive reinforcement
- Know de-escalation techniques
- Document incidents

## Communication with Children
- Use age-appropriate language
- Be patient and calm
- Give clear, simple instructions
- Acknowledge good behavior
- Build positive relationships
key_points: Children under 4 require child safety seat
Children 4-8 require booster seat by law
Danger zone extends 10 feet around bus
Count children before moving bus
Secure wheelchairs with approved restraints

units.title: Loading & Unloading Procedures
units.content:
# Loading & Unloading Procedures

## Danger Zone Awareness

### 10-Foot Danger Zone
- Area around bus where children are at risk
- Driver may not see children in this zone
- Most fatalities occur here
- Children must stay 10 feet away
- Count children until all are clear

### Blind Spots
- Directly in front of bus (10 feet)
- Directly behind bus
- Along sides near rear wheels
- Mirror blind spots
- Check all mirrors before moving

## Morning Loading Procedure

### Before Opening Door
1. Stop with front of bus ahead of children
2. Set parking brake
3. Open door only after children are in view
4. Ensure hazard lights flashing
5. Count children as they board

### During Loading
1. Children board one at a time
2. Watch each child step onto bus
3. Count as each child boards
4. Ensure children sit immediately
5. Count again before closing door

## Afternoon Unloading Procedure

### Before Opening Door
1. Stop at designated area
2. Set parking brake
3. Activate flashing lights
4. Extend stop arm (if equipped)
5. Check all mirrors

### During Unloading
1. Children exit one at a time
2. Watch each child step off
3. Ensure children move away from bus
4. Count children as they exit
5. Wait until all children clear

## Crossing Procedures

### Safe Crossing
1. Driver must see child cross
2. Use hand signals to direct children
3. Ensure no traffic approaching
4. Children walk, never run
5. Children stop at edge of bus

### Driver Responsibilities
1. Signal children when safe to cross
2. Watch until child reaches other side
3. Count crossing children
4. Never allow children to retrieve dropped items
5. Child should tell driver if something dropped

## Late Children
- Never wait beyond scheduled time
- Document late arrival
- Communicate with school/parents
- Follow district policy
- Don't leave unsupervised children
key_points: 10-foot danger zone around bus is highest risk area
Count children as they board and exit
Use hand signals to direct crossing children
Never move bus if child is near
Children should never retrieve dropped items near bus

units.title: Student Behaviour Management
units.content:
# Student Behaviour Management

## Understanding Child Behavior

### Age Groups
- Younger children (4-7): Need clear rules, easily distracted
- Middle years (8-11): Testing boundaries, peer influence
- Older children (12+): More independent, may challenge authority
- Mixed ages: Different needs and expectations

### Common Issues
- Excessive noise
- Standing/moving while bus moving
- Not staying seated
- Distracting driver
- Bullying or teasing

## Positive Behavior Strategies

### Proactive Approaches
- Establish clear rules at start
- Use positive reinforcement
- Assign seats strategically
- Greet children by name
- Build relationships

### Rules to Communicate
- Stay seated while bus moving
- Keep hands to yourself
- Speak quietly
- Follow driver instructions
- No eating or drinking

## Seat Assignment Strategy
- Younger children near front
- Separate known conflicts
- Problem students near driver
- Buddies for support needs
- Regular review of assignments

## Verbal Interventions

### Calm Approach
- Use calm, firm voice
- Address behavior, not child
- Give clear choices
- Use "when/then" statements
- Avoid power struggles

### Redirecting Behavior
- Give specific instructions
- Acknowledge compliance
- Use non-verbal signals
- Change seat if needed
- Contact school if persistent

## Documentation
- Record behavior incidents
- Include date, time, description
- Note witnesses
- Report to school and parents
- Keep records for patterns

## Escalation Procedures
- Attempt de-escalation
- Never engage physically
- Contact school administration
- Pull over if necessary
- Request support if needed

## Working with School
- Regular communication with teachers
- Attend training sessions
- Report concerns early
- Follow school policies
- Coordinate behavior plans
key_points: Establish clear rules at beginning of year
Use calm voice and positive reinforcement
Assign seats strategically to manage behavior
Document all behavior incidents
Never engage physically with students

units.title: Emergency Evacuation Drills
units.content:
# Emergency Evacuation Drills

## Emergency Exits

### Types of Exits
- Front door (primary exit)
- Rear emergency door
- Windows (push-out or break glass)
- Roof hatches (some buses)
- Side emergency doors

### Exit Operation
- Know how to open each exit
- Practice opening regularly
- Ensure handles/mechanisms work
- Train on emergency release
- Mark exits clearly

## Evacuation Procedures

### Driver Responsibilities
1. Stop bus safely
2. Set parking brake
3. Assess situation
4. Decide best exit to use
5. Instruct older students to assist
6. Lead evacuation
7. Account for all students

### Student Instructions
- Remain calm
- Listen to driver
- Exit quickly but safely
- Move away from bus
- Stay together as group
- Don't retrieve belongings

## Emergency Equipment

### Fire Extinguisher
- Know location
- Check pressure gauge monthly
- Know how to use (PASS method)
- Replace if used or expired
- Train all drivers

### First Aid Kit
- Know location
- Check contents regularly
- Replace used items
- Have emergency numbers
- Basic training for all drivers

### Emergency Hammer
- For breaking windows
- Know location
- Practice on training window
- Check regularly
- Replace if missing

## Evacuation Scenarios

### Fire Evacuation
1. Stop immediately
2. Turn off engine
3. Lead evacuation
4. Use nearest exit
5. Move upwind
6. Call emergency services

### Accident Evacuation
1. Assess safety of exit
2. If safe, evacuate through doors
3. If unsafe, use windows
4. Move away from road
5. Account for all students
6. Keep group together

## Drill Requirements
- Required number of drills per year
- Document each drill
- Time the evacuation
- Evaluate performance
- Retrain as needed
- Involve school administrators

## Post-Evacuation
- Count students multiple times
- Keep group together
- Move further away if danger
- Contact dispatch
- Await emergency services
- Don't re-enter bus
key_points: Know location and operation of all emergency exits
Driver leads evacuation and accounts for all students
Practice drills regularly and document results
Know fire extinguisher location and operation
Never re-enter bus after evacuation

units.title: First Aid for Children
units.content:
# First Aid for Children

## Emergency Assessment

### Primary Survey (Dr. ABC)
- **Danger**: Check for danger to self, child, others
- **Response**: Is child conscious? (shake and shout)
- **Airway**: Is airway clear? (head tilt, chin lift)
- **Breathing**: Look, listen, feel for 10 seconds
- **Circulation**: Check pulse and severe bleeding

### When to Call Emergency Services
- Unconsciousness
- Not breathing
- Severe bleeding
- Possible spinal injury
- Seizure
- Severe allergic reaction

## Child CPR

### Chest Compressions
- 30 compressions at 100-120 per minute
- Depth: 5 cm (2 inches)
- Hand placement: Center of chest
- One hand for small children
- Two hands for larger children

### Rescue Breaths
- 2 rescue breaths
- Tilt head, lift chin
- Pinch nose (for older children)
- Seal mouth over child's mouth
- Watch for chest rise

### Continue CPR
- Until child responds
- Until AED arrives
- Until professional help takes over
- Switch every 2 minutes if fatigued

## Choking Response

### Signs of Choking
- Unable to speak or cry
- Difficulty breathing
- Clutching throat
- Turning blue
- Loss of consciousness

### Conscious Child
1. Ask "Are you choking?"
2. Get consent
3. Stand behind child
4. Place fist above navel
5. Perform abdominal thrusts
6. Continue until object expelled

### Unconscious Child
1. Lower to ground
2. Call for help
3. Begin CPR
4. Check mouth for object before breaths
5. Continue CPR

## Common Childhood Emergencies

### Allergic Reactions
- Recognize signs (rash, swelling, breathing difficulty)
- Locate epinephrine if prescribed
- Administer if trained
- Call emergency services
- Keep child calm

### Seizures
- Protect from injury
- Time the seizure
- Don't restrain
- Don't put anything in mouth
- Roll to side when possible
- Call emergency if prolonged

### Asthma Attack
- Help sit upright
- Locate inhaler
- Administer medication
- Call for help if no improvement
- Monitor breathing

## First Aid Kit for School Transport

### Required Items
- Adhesive bandages (various sizes)
- Gauze pads and roller bandages
- Adhesive tape
- Antiseptic wipes
- Disposable gloves
- Scissors
- Emergency blanket
- Cold packs
- CPR mask
- Emergency contact information

### Medication Policies
- Only administer with authorization
- Know which students have conditions
- Store medications properly
- Follow school protocols
- Document any administration
key_points: Primary survey: Danger, Response, Airway, Breathing, Circulation
Child CPR: 30 compressions, 2 breaths, 5 cm depth
Abdominal thrusts for conscious choking child
Never restrain during seizure, roll to side
Know which students have medical conditions

units.title: Route Risk Assessment
units.content:
# Route Risk Assessment

## Pre-Route Assessment

### Road Conditions
- Identify dangerous intersections
- Note narrow roads
- Watch for steep hills
- Check for poor visibility areas
- Identify railroad crossings

### Stops Assessment
- Safe pick-up/drop-off locations
- Adequate visibility at stops
- Away from blind curves
- Not too close to intersections
- Safe pedestrian crossing areas

## Daily Route Checks

### Morning Check
- Drive route before first pickup
- Note construction zones
- Check for road hazards
- Identify any changes since yesterday
- Adjust timing accordingly

### Weather Considerations
- Rain makes roads slippery
- Fog reduces visibility
- Flooding may require route changes
- Ice (in cold areas)
- Alternative route for severe weather

## School Zone Safety

### Speed Management
- Observe school zone speed limits
- Reduce speed during arrival/dismissal
- 25 km/h in active school zones
- Watch for crossing guards
- Yield to pedestrians

### High-Risk Areas
- Areas with speeding traffic
- Poorly lit areas
- Heavy pedestrian traffic
- Known accident locations
- Areas with history of incidents

## Hazard Communication

### Reporting System
- Document all hazards found
- Photograph when possible
- Report to supervisor
- Track until resolved
- Coordinate with local authorities

### Temporary Hazards
- Construction zones
- Special events
- Road repairs
- Downed trees/power lines
- Flooded roads

## Route Review Process

### Annual Review
- Evaluate entire route yearly
- Involve drivers in review
- Check all stop locations
- Assess road changes
- Update maps

### Parent Communication
- Notify of major route changes
- Provide alternate stop options
- Respond to safety concerns
- Document all communication
- Follow school policies
key_points: Identify dangerous intersections and hazards before route
Drive route daily to check for new hazards
Observe school zone speed limits (25 km/h)
Document and report all hazards found
Review routes annually with driver input

units.title: Vehicle Inspection for School Transport
units.content:
# Vehicle Inspection for School Transport

## Daily Pre-Trip Inspection

### Exterior Check
- Tyre pressure and tread depth
- All lights and signals functional
- Stop arm operation (if equipped)
- Emergency exit operation
- Clean all mirrors and windows
- Check for body damage

### Interior Check
- All seat belts functional
- Emergency exits accessible
- Fire extinguisher present and charged
- First aid kit complete
- Emergency equipment in place
- No loose items that could become projectiles

## Weekly Checks

### Student Items
- Clean interior thoroughly
- Check under seats for lost items
- Disinfect high-touch surfaces
- Empty trash
- Report damaged seats

### Systems Check
- Brake operation
- Steering response
- Warning lights
- Horn operation
- Heater/defroster function

## Monthly Inspection

### Detailed Inspection
- Brake pads and rotors
- Suspension components
- Exhaust system
- Belts and hoses
- Fluid levels (oil, coolant, brake)

### Safety Equipment
- Fire extinguisher pressure
- First aid kit contents
- Emergency hammer present
- Reflector triangles
- Complete spare tyre kit

## Documenting Inspections

### Daily Log
- Date and odometer reading
- Any defects found
- Repairs made
- Driver signature
- Management review

### Defect Reporting
- Immediate reporting for safety issues
- Written report for all defects
- Do not drive with safety defects
- Track repair completion
- Follow up on outstanding issues

## Stop Arm System

### Operation Check
- Test daily
- Ensure lights flash
- Arm extends fully
- Sound warning if equipped
- Report issues immediately

### Legal Requirements
- Must stop for loading/unloading
- Activate before opening door
- Don't move until arm retracts
- Cameras optional but recommended
- Document all activations

## Student Tracking System

### Paper Systems
- Daily attendance log
- Boarding/alighting checklist
- Emergency contact sheet
- Medical information accessible
- Sign-in/sign-out procedures

### Electronic Systems
- RFID card readers
- GPS tracking
- Parent notification apps
- Driver tablet for attendance
- Real-time location sharing

## End of Day Check

### After Last Drop-off
- Check for sleeping children
- Search all seats
- Look under seats
- Check emergency exits
- Report any lost items
- Document any incidents

### Secure Vehicle
- Close all windows
- Lock all doors
- Turn off lights
- Remove keys
- Park in designated area
key_points: Complete pre-trip inspection before every route
Never drive with safety defects
Test stop arm operation daily
Check for sleeping children after last drop-off
Document all inspections and defects
